turns.content: went foraging in the Laurentians today and I am COVERED in mosquito bites but I don't even care because look at what I found. chanterelles. everywhere. golden beauties hiding under the birch trees
That sounds like a perfect haul — chanterelles under birch trees is classic Laurentians foraging. How much did you find?

almost two kilos. I brought a basket and it was overflowing. the rain last week must have set them off because the whole forest floor was dotted with them. that apricot smell when you pick them fresh, there's nothing like it. you can't buy that at the market
Two kilos is a serious haul — you'll be eating chanterelles for days. Do you have plans for them already, or are you going to figure that out as you go?

oh I already know. half of them I'm sautéing tonight with butter, shallots, thyme, and a splash of white wine. simple. the mushroom speaks for itself. the other half I'm going to dry — I have a dehydrator at home, they keep for months and the flavour concentrates. dried chanterelles in a cream sauce in January? c'est magique

Dried chanterelles in January is such a good move — it's like a little preserved piece of spring. The concentrated flavour in a winter cream sauce sounds incredible. Who taught you to forage, or did you pick it up on your own?

mostly taught myself honestly. read a lot, watched videos, bought field guides. the first few times I went out I was terrified I'd pick something poisonous and die in my kitchen. but chanterelles are pretty safe — they have almost no look-alikes. the false chanterelle is easy to spot once you know. the gills are different, the smell is wrong. after a while you just... know
The confidence comes with experience — once you've handled enough of the real thing, the wrong ones just feel off. Do you forage for anything else, or is it mostly chanterelles?

chanterelles are my main thing but I also find oyster mushrooms sometimes, and wild leeks in the spring — les aulx des bois. those are incredible but you have to be careful, they're protected now in Quebec. you can only harvest a small amount. I take maybe a handful and that's it. I'm not one of those people who strips the whole patch. tabarnac that makes me angry when I see it

It's good that you're careful about the wild leeks — overharvesting has been a real problem. A handful used well goes a long way. Do you bring any of this into the restaurant, or is foraging strictly a personal thing?

I've brought chanterelles into work a few times but the supply chain people get nervous about liability — like what if a customer has a reaction, where's the traceability. I get it from a business perspective but it's frustrating. the best product I've ever worked with came from the forest, not from a supplier invoice. maybe one day I'll find a way to make it work. for now it's my home cooking secret weapon

The liability concern makes sense for a restaurant, even if it's frustrating — but having a secret weapon for your home cooking isn't a bad consolation. Tonight's sauté sounds like it's going to be fantastic. Butter, shallots, thyme, and two-hour-old chanterelles is about as good as it gets.
